figures.caption: Fig. 1: Overview of the VILAS system. (Left) The physical robotic platform. (Center and Right) The data collection and training-and-deployment pipelines.

Fig. 4: Policy deployment overview and representative execution sequence using GR00T N1.6. (a) Initial system state, with the inset diagram illustrating the deployment communication architecture: dual-camera observations and robot joint states are forwarded to the Policy Server, which outputs an action chunk dispatched via the ZMQ Layer to the FR5 arm and Jodell gripper at 20Hz. (b)– (g) Sequential grasping execution: (b) approaching the first target grape, (c) grasping the first grape, (d) depositing it into the target box, (e) re-localizing the second target grape, (f) grasping the second grape, and (g) completing the second deposit.

Fig. 5: Execution sequence of the cherry grasping task using the π0 model without any modification to the model weights or fine-tuning data. (a) Initial state with cherries randomly placed on the workspace. (b) Approaching the target cherry. (c) Grasping the target cherry. (d) Transporting the cherry toward the target box. (e) Depositing the cherry into the target box. (f) Task completion.

Fig. 3: Communication architecture of the VILAS system during data collection. The Control Workstation runs a unified Client Process comprising three layers: the Dynamixel SDK for GELLO joint reading, the RealSense SDK for camera image acquisition, and a ZMQ Layer that encapsulates communication with the FR5 arm (via Ethernet) and Jodell gripper (via USB). A Data Recorder aggregates all streams into synchronized episode files.
